After a focused sprint of fixes, refactoring, and documentation work, Diogenes 1.0.17 is live. This update is one of the most important since the project launched, not because of new features, but because of how much more stable, compatible, and accessible the ecosystem became.

Two small but meaningful releases back to back. 1.0.18 simplifies the server configuration system, and 1.0.19 fixes a critical SDK bug affecting servers running multiple licensed plugins simultaneously.

A lot happened since the last devlog. 1.0.20 ships the most important fix in the project's history, the Agora community server is now live, and the myPlugins storefront is officially open. Let's break it all down.
Welcome to the official Diogenes DevLog. This is where we will document the evolution of our licensing ecosystem.

Integrating the Diogenes SDK into your software is the final step to ensure total protection. Our SDK handles secure communication, hardware fingerprinting (HWID), and real-time validation with your server.

Understanding how Diogenes protects your software is crucial. Our security model relies on a multi-stage validation process that binds a license to specific hardware and ensures the integrity of every request.
The Diogenes Bot is the primary interface for managing your licensing system. It uses Slash Commands (/) for a modern and intuitive experience.

The Diogenes Server is the central brain of the ecosystem. Built with Ktor and Netty, it acts as a high-performance API that handles license validation requests, manages the MongoDB database, and powers the Discord Bot integration.
Welcome to the official Diogenes documentation. Diogenes is a high-performance licensing and security ecosystem designed for developers who demand total control and robust protection.
You don't need React to write simple standalone pages.
March 13, 2026 · 5 min read Eduardo Felleto Head Developer
March 12, 2026 · 3 min read Eduardo Felleto Head Developer
March 11, 2026 · 4 min read Eduardo Felleto Head Developer
March 10, 2026 · One min read Eduardo Felleto Head Developer
Sinope Layer: Advanced obfuscation for JAR files. Stay tuned for more updates!

After a focused sprint of fixes, refactoring, and documentation work, Diogenes 1.0.17 is live. This update is one of the most important since the project launched, not because of new features, but because of how much more stable, compatible, and accessible the ecosystem became. Here's everything that changed.

The biggest change in this release: the SDK now supports Java 8 and above. Previously, the SDK required Java 21, which excluded a significant portion of the Minecraft server ecosystem, especially older 1.8.x servers that still run on legacy JDKs. Starting from 1.0.17, the SDK is fully compatible with: Java Version Supported Java 8 ✅ Java 11 ✅ Java 17 ✅ Java 21 ✅ This change required a complete rework of how the fat JAR is assembled. More on that below:

This release resolves three critical issues that were introduced during the Java 8 migration. They appeared sequentially — each fix revealed the next — but all share the same root cause: how Kotlin libraries were being bundled and relocated inside the SDK JAR. Fix #1 — ExceptionInInitializerError on authentication (1.0.15 → 1.0.16) What happened: The kotlin-reflect library was being relocated inside the fat JAR (e.g. kotlin.* → dev.efelleto.diogenes.libs.kotlin.*). This broke an internal mechanism in kotlin-reflect that loads built-in type metadata from .kotlin_builtins resource files using hardcoded classpath paths. Error: Caused by: java.lang.IllegalStateException: Resource not found in classpath: dev/efelleto/diogenes/libs/kotlin/...kotlin_builtins Fix: kotlin-reflect is now fully excluded from the fat JAR. Since it modifies internal JVM behavior and relies on hardcoded resource paths, it cannot be safely relocated. Fix #2 — ClassNotFoundException: kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope (1.0.16 → 1.0.17) What happened: After excluding kotlin-reflect, kotlinx-coroutines was also missing from the JAR. The Ktor CIO engine depends heavily on coroutines, and since the target servers (1.8.8 vanilla) don't provide Kotlin at runtime, the class was simply not found. Error: java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope Fix: kotlinx-coroutines-core is now bundled inside the fat JAR without relocation, ensuring it's always available regardless of the server environment. Fix #3 — Banner log pollution on server startup (1.0.17) What happened: The Diogenes ASCII banner was being printed inside a runTaskLater (10 tick delay), which caused Spigot's own startup messages to interleave with the banner output in the console. Before: __________________________________________________________________________________ _____^_ ... __________________________________________________________________________________ [INFO]: The server will be available in 4 seconds. ← intruder [INFO]: The server will be available in 3 seconds. ← intruder [INFO]: AUTH: Successfully authenticated. After: __________________________________________________________________________________ _____^_ ... __________________________________________________________________________________ [INFO]: The server will be available in 4 seconds. [INFO]: The server will be available in 3 seconds. [INFO]: AUTH: Successfully authenticated. Fix: The banner is now printed synchronously during onEnable(), before any async task is scheduled.

The entire fat JAR assembly strategy was redesigned. Here's the final configuration that makes everything work cleanly: Library Strategy Reason kotlin-stdlib Bundled, not relocated Server doesn't provide it kotlin-reflect Excluded Breaks with any relocation kotlinx-coroutines Bundled, not relocated Required by Ktor CIO kotlinx.atomicfu Bundled + relocated Transitive dep, may conflict io.ktor Bundled + relocated Avoid classpath conflicts com.google.gson Bundled + relocated Avoid classpath conflicts

All documentation pages were reviewed and updated to reflect the current state of the project: Getting Started: Java requirement corrected to Java 8+, Gradle snippet fixed to proper Kotlin DSL syntax. Integration: Fully rewritten with a parameter reference table, step-by-step authentication flow, and a complete error handling table. Installation: Fixed inconsistency between config.yml and settings.yml references. Configuration block is now a proper YAML example. Discord Commands: Fixed header hierarchy and standardized formatting. Security Flow: Minor formatting cleanup.

The Diogenes Discord server is being set up. It will be the central hub for: Getting help with installation and integration Reporting bugs and suggesting features Following development updates in real time Connecting with other developers using the ecosystem An invite link will be shared here and in the README as soon as the server is ready. Stay tuned.

Sinope Layer: JAR obfuscation CLI tool. The groundwork is laid, active development starts next. Discord invite: Community server going live soon. Thanks for following the project. If Diogenes has been useful to you, consider leaving a ⭐ on GitHub, it helps a lot. — Eduardo Felleto

Two small but meaningful releases back to back. 1.0.18 simplifies the server configuration system, and 1.0.19 fixes a critical SDK bug affecting servers running multiple licensed plugins simultaneously.

Previously, the server generated a nested folder structure on first run: diogenes-config/ ├── server/ │ └── config.yml └── discord/ └── messages.yml This made sense in theory, but in practice it was unnecessary overhead. The messages.yml file contained Discord embed messages that are better handled at the Sinope layer, and the extra folders added friction for anyone setting up the server quickly.

Starting from 1.0.18, the server generates a single file at the root, right next to the JAR: DiogenesServer.jar config.yml ← generated here on first run The config file itself was also cleaned up. Two unused fields (admin-role-id and log-channel-id) were removed: server: port: 8080 mongo-uri: "mongodb://localhost:27017" discord: bot-token: "YOUR_BOT_TOKEN_HERE"

The first-run console output now follows the same visual standard as the rest of the server logs: Before: [!] Config folder created on: /home/user/diogenes-config [!] Please fill the information in ./diogenes-config/server/config.yml and restart. After: [ INFO ] CONFIG: File created at: /home/user/config.yml [ INFO ] CONFIG: Please fill config.yml and restart the server.

If you are upgrading from a previous version, your old diogenes-config/server/config.yml will not be read automatically. Before restarting with 1.0.18: Copy your mongo-uri and bot-token values from the old file. Delete the diogenes-config/ folder. Run the new JAR — it will generate a fresh config.yml at the root. Paste your credentials and restart.

When two or more plugins using the Diogenes SDK were loaded on the same server, they shared the same SDK instance — because DiogenesSDK was implemented as a Kotlin object (singleton). This caused the second plugin to overwrite the pluginFolder of the first, making both plugins read from the same license.yml file. In practice, this meant the second plugin could authenticate using the first plugin's key without having its own license configured. [ INFO ] AUTH: Successfully authenticated. PRODUCT: plugin1 ← correct [ INFO ] AUTH: Successfully authenticated. PRODUCT: plugin2 ← wrong, used plugin1's key

DiogenesSDK was refactored from an object to a class. Each init() call now creates an isolated instance with its own productId, baseUrl, and pluginFolder. plugins/Plugin1/license.yml ← read by plugin1's instance plugins/Plugin2/license.yml ← read by plugin2's instance The public API is unchanged — your onEnable() code stays exactly the same: DiogenesSDK.init(this, "your-product-id", "http://your-server:8080", () -> { ... });

The ASCII banner now prints together with the authentication result, instead of appearing separately at the top of the log: __________________________________________________________________________________ _____^_ ... / \_/ 0 \ Diogenes 1.0.19 [ INFO ] AUTH: Successfully authenticated. PRODUCT: plugin __________________________________________________________________________________ If multiple plugins are loaded, only the first one prints the banner. Subsequent plugins print only their result line — keeping the console clean. — Eduardo Felleto

A lot happened since the last devlog. 1.0.20 ships the most important fix in the project's history, the Agora community server is now live, and the myPlugins storefront is officially open. Let's break it all down.

If you've been using Diogenes for a while, you may have noticed an annoying problem: valid licenses occasionally triggering a hardware mismatch after a reboot, a system update, or a simple user switch. This is known as a False Positive: The system blocking a legitimate user for no real reason. Version 1.0.20 eliminates this entirely. The full change is in SecurityUtils.kt.

The old HWID was built using data that changes without the user doing anything: user.name: if the server was started as root and then as a regular user, the hash changed. os.version: a kernel update or Windows patch was enough to break the license. Network interface ordering: Java reads network adapters in the order the OS delivers them. On reboot, if Wi-Fi responded 1ms before Ethernet, the MAC address list inverted and the hash changed. All of these caused real users to get locked out of plugins they legitimately purchased.

🔧 Sorted MAC List Previously, MAC addresses were appended directly as the OS returned them: // before — order-dependent, breaks on reboot hwData.append(mac.joinToString("") { "%02x".format(it) }) Now they are collected into a list, sorted alphabetically, then appended: // after — stable regardless of boot order macList.add(mac.joinToString("") { "%02x".format(it) }) macList.sort() macList.forEach { hwData.append(it) } Also added explicit filtering for VirtualBox and Docker interfaces, which change on every container restart: if (netInterface.isLoopback || netInterface.isVirtual || netInterface.name.contains("vboxnet") || netInterface.name.contains("docker")) continue 🔧 Removal of volatile variables user.name and os.version have been removed from the HWID calculation entirely. The system now keeps only stable, hardware-level properties: // removed — volatile hwData.append(System.getProperty("os.version")) hwData.append(System.getProperty("user.name")) // kept — stable hwData.append(System.getProperty("os.name")) hwData.append(System.getProperty("os.arch")) hwData.append(Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()) 🔧 Linux machine-id On Linux, the SDK now reads /etc/machine-id, a file generated at OS installation time that acts as a permanent unique identifier. It survives reboots, user switches, and system updates: if (System.getProperty("os.name").lowercase().contains("linux")) { val machineId = File("/etc/machine-id") if (machineId.exists()) hwData.append(machineId.readText().trim()) } 🔧 macOS IOPlatformUUID On macOS, the SDK now captures the IOPlatformUUID directly from the system's hardware registry. This identifies the logic board, making it virtually impossible to spoof without physically replacing hardware: } else if (System.getProperty("os.name").lowercase().contains("mac")) { try { val process = Runtime.getRuntime().exec("ioreg -rd1 -c IOPlatformExpertDevice") val uuid = process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText() .split("IOPlatformUUID")[1] .split("\"")[2] hwData.append(uuid) } catch (e: Exception) {} }

Scenario Before 1.0.20 After 1.0.20 Server reboot ⚠️ Possible mismatch ✅ Stable Kernel / OS update ⚠️ Possible mismatch ✅ Stable Running as root vs user ⚠️ Possible mismatch ✅ Stable Network adapter reorder ⚠️ Possible mismatch ✅ Stable VirtualBox / Docker interfaces ⚠️ Possible mismatch ✅ Filtered out The Diogenes SDK now has a hardware memory that is stable, fair, and robust. You can sell licenses with confidence knowing that your customers won't be locked out of software they paid for.

The official Diogenes community server, Agora, is now open. Agora is built around mutual help and knowledge sharing. Whether you're setting up your first Diogenes server, debugging an integration, or just want to follow the project's progress, this is the place. What you'll find: #announcements and #updates to stay on top of every release #faq with answers to the most common questions #community-help for support from the community #contact via ModMail for private or commercial inquiries Volunteer moderator applications will be opening soon. If you're active in the community and want to help keep Agora a great place, stay tuned to #announcements. Join the Community Click here to join the Agora Discord Server

Diogenes is and will always be free and open-source. No ads, no subscriptions, no hidden fees. If the project has been useful to you, you can now support its development through Ko-fi or PIX. Every contribution, no matter the size, helps keep the ecosystem alive and growing. Supporters receive: Supporter role in Agora Access to the exclusive #supporters chat Access to #colors to set a custom name color via Jeb BOT Discount on myPlugins products Priority support in #contact Your name in the project credits on GitHub

myPlugins is the storefront for plugins built, tested, and protected with Diogenes. Every plugin available through myPlugins is production-ready and comes with the same infrastructure standard you already know from the Diogenes ecosystem. Custom development is also available, open a thread in #contact to discuss your project. Browse available products in the #products channel inside Agora. — Eduardo Felleto

Welcome to the official Diogenes DevLog. This is where we will document the evolution of our licensing ecosystem. Today marks a major milestone as we've finalized the documentation for the Server and SDK modules. Our goal is to provide a seamless and secure experience for developers who want to protect their intellectual property without the headache of complex integrations.
Sinope Layer: Advanced obfuscation for JAR files. Stay tuned for more updates!

Integrating the Diogenes SDK into your software is the final step to ensure total protection. Our SDK handles secure communication, hardware fingerprinting (HWID), and real-time validation with your server.
First, ensure you have added the JitPack repository and the SDK dependency to your project as shown in the Getting Started section.
To start using the licensing system, call DiogenesSDK.init() inside your plugin's onEnable(). This method manages the entire authentication flow automatically.

DiogenesSDK.init(Plugin plugin, String productId, String baseUrl, Runnable onSuccess) Parameter Type Description plugin Plugin Your plugin instance (this). Used to schedule async tasks and disable the plugin on failure. productId String The product identifier registered in your Discord Server. Must match exactly. baseUrl String The full URL of your running Diogenes Server (e.g. http://your-server:8080). onSuccess Runnable Callback executed only if the license is valid. Place all your plugin logic here.

@Override public void onEnable() { DiogenesSDK.init(this, "my-product-id", "http://your-server:8080", () -> { getServer().getPluginManager().registerEvents(new MyListener(), this); getCommand("mycommand").setExecutor(new MyCommand()); }); }
override fun onEnable() { DiogenesSDK.init(this, "your-product-id", "http://your-server:8080") { isAuthorized.set(true) server.pluginManager.registerEvents(this, this) } }

When init() is called, the SDK handles everything automatically: The license.yml file is created in your plugin's data folder on first run. The player fills in their license key and restarts the server. The SDK reads the key, generates a Hardware ID (HWID), and sends a validation request to your server. If the license is valid, the onSuccess callback is triggered on the main thread. If validation fails for any reason, the plugin is automatically disabled.

You don't need to handle errors manually, the SDK does it for you. The table below shows every possible outcome: Scenario SDK Behavior Console Output License valid Executes onSuccess callback AUTH: Successfully authenticated. license.yml not found Creates the file and disables plugin License file created. Please fill it and restart. Key is blank or placeholder Disables plugin Please provide a valid license key in license.yml. Invalid key / HWID mismatch Disables plugin Server-provided error message Server unreachable Disables plugin ERROR: Remote server unreachable. caution Always place your plugin's core logic (event listeners, commands, tasks) inside the onSuccess callback. Code placed outside of it will run regardless of license validation.

Understanding how Diogenes protects your software is crucial. Our security model relies on a multi-stage validation process that binds a license to specific hardware and ensures the integrity of every request.

When DiogenesSDK.init() is called, the following sequence occurs: Hardware Fingerprinting: The SDK scans unique hardware identifiers (CPU, Motherboard, Disk UUID) to generate a persistent Hardware ID (HWID). Encrypted Payload: The License Key and HWID are bundled into an encrypted request sent to your Diogenes Server. Database Validation: The server queries the MongoDB cluster to verify: Is the key active? Does the product ID match? Is the HWID either empty (first use) or a perfect match with the stored one? Authorization Callback: If all checks pass, the server sends a signed success signal. Only then does the SDK trigger the code inside your initialization callback.
The Security Flow is designed to prevent common cracking techniques:

A license key is locked to the first machine that redeems it. Attempt: A user shares their key with a friend. Result: The server detects a HWID mismatch and returns an error, preventing the plugin from enabling on the second machine.

By placing your core logic (events, commands, tasks) inside the SDK callback, you create a "Locked Room" scenario. Manual Bypass: Even if a cracker forces the isAuthorized variable to true, the code inside the callback was never triggered by the SDK engine, leaving the software in a hollow state.
The SDK is a messenger; the Server is the judge. No sensitive validation logic happens purely on the client side, making it significantly harder to manipulate via local memory editing.

State SDK Action Software Result Authorized Executes callback Full access Invalid Key Ignores callback Plugin remains disabled HWID Mismatch Ignores callback Error log sent to Admin Audit Server Offline Failsafe (Denied) Plugin remains disabled Note on Sinope For even higher security, we recommend using the Sinope Layer to obfuscate the strings and methods mentioned in this flow, preventing static analysis of your compiled JAR.
The Diogenes Bot is the primary interface for managing your licensing system. It uses Slash Commands (/) for a modern and intuitive experience.
These commands are restricted to users with Administrator permissions on the server.
Description: Registers a new product into the database. id: The internal identifier (e.g., pluginpvp). name: The friendly display name (e.g., Plugin PvP).
Description: Generates a new unique license key for a specific product. product: The ID of the product you want to generate the key for.
Description: Lists all active customers currently registered in the database and the products they own.
Description: Lists all generated license keys, showing their current status (Active/Redeemed/Expired).
Description: Configures the security channel where the bot will send action logs and security alerts.
These commands are available to all users or customers interacting with the bot.
Description: Opens an interactive product management panel where users can see available software and details.
Description: Allows a customer to activate their license key and bind it to their account/hardware. key: The license key received (Format: XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX).

Command Permission Purpose /create-product Administrator Register software in the DB /create-license Administrator Generate a new serial key /browse-customers Administrator List users and their products /browse-licenses Administrator View all keys in the system /audit-setup Administrator Set up security log channel /browse-products Public View available products /redeem Public Activate a license key Developer Note The bot status is automatically set to Playing: Server running upon successful connection. If the commands do not appear in your Discord client immediately, try restarting your Discord or check if the Bot Token has the applications.commands scope enabled in the Developer Portal.

The Diogenes Server is the central brain of the ecosystem. Built with Ktor and Netty, it acts as a high-performance API that handles license validation requests, manages the MongoDB database, and powers the Discord Bot integration.

You don't need to compile the code yourself. We provide ready-to-use binaries for every stable release. Go to the Diogenes Releases page. Download the DiogenesServer.jar file. Place the .jar file in a dedicated folder on your VPS or local machine.

Open your terminal or command prompt inside the folder where you placed the file and run: java -jar DiogenesServer.jar info On the first run, the server will automatically generate a settings.yml file and then shut down. You must configure this file before the server can start successfully.

Open the generated settings.yml and fill in your credentials: port: 8080 # The port where the API will listen mongo-uri: "" # Your MongoDB connection string bot-token: "" # Your Discord Bot token from the Developer Portal
Running a backend server can lead to a few common "hiccups." Use this guide to identify and fix issues quickly based on the terminal logs.

Error: The server starts and immediately closes without showing the ASCII banner. Fix: Ensure settings.yml exists in the root folder. Diogenes requires a valid bot-token and mongo-uri to boot. If these fields are empty, the server will shut down for security reasons.

Error: [ERROR] FATAL: Failed to initialize services followed by an Address already in use message. Fix: Another application is already using port 8080. Open settings.yml and change the port value to a different one (e.g., 8081 or 8888), then restart the server.

Error: The console hangs at [INFO] DATABASE: Connecting to MongoDB... for a long time. Fix: Check if your MongoDB service is running. If you are using MongoDB Atlas, ensure your server's IP is whitelisted in the "Network Access" tab. Also, verify that your mongo-uri includes the correct credentials.

Error: [ERROR] JDA: Starting Discord Bot Manager... followed by an authentication error. Fix: Your Discord token is likely invalid or has been reset. Go to the Discord Developer Portal, reset your Bot Token, and update your settings.yml.

Once the server is running and the Diogenes Banner appears, you can verify the connection status manually: Access: Open your browser and navigate to http://your-server-ip:8080/. Response: You should see the message: [+] Diogenes API is Running!. Verification: This confirms the Netty engine is live and ready to process requests from the SDK. Note on VPS If you are running on a remote server, make sure the port is open in your firewall (UFW/iptables), otherwise the SDK will not be able to connect.

Welcome to the official Diogenes documentation. Diogenes is a high-performance licensing and security ecosystem designed for developers who demand total control and robust protection. Pro Tip Always keep your Diogenes Server updated to the latest version to ensure you have the most recent security patches and features.
To integrate Diogenes into your project, you'll need to use our SDK provided via JitPack.
Java 8 or higher. A running instance of MongoDB. A Discord Bot Token (for notifications and management).

Add the JitPack repository and the Diogenes dependency to your build.gradle.kts: repositories { maven("https://jitpack.io") } dependencies { implementation("com.github.efelleto.Diogenes:SDK:{version}") }

Add the JitPack repository and the Diogenes dependency to your pom.xml: <repositories> <repository> <id>jitpack.io</id> <url>https://jitpack.io</url> </repository> </repositories> <dependency> <groupId>com.github.efelleto.Diogenes</groupId> <artifactId>SDK</artifactId> <version>{version}</version> </dependency>
Markdown page example You don't need React to write simple standalone pages.
